author: @sk8pkl
text: what if you run your graphene ink onto your graphene surface to see if it generates voltage?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am not sure why i would do that mate all that would happen is i would get a build up of dried ink even if it did create a voltage - which it isn't likely to do as it is non polar

replies.author: @sk8pkl
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith oohhh, so you need a liquid wich its molecules are like magnets... i c. And why is the copper cloride helping the reaction? only because of the ions?? would a solution of water and cloridric acid work? (the acid that has gold ions in it... not sure it is called like this...)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
Not magnetic mate - ionic - they have to carry a charge when they impact the surface this charge is transferred. Any ionic fluid will do it and there is a long long list to try before finding a good one - that's a lot of experiment

replies.text: oohh i see.... thankyou

replies.text:
sk8pkl
no worries smate

author: @FredGandt
text:
With such a dramatically _better_ result using copper chloride, it's difficult to ignore, but the abundance and ease of simple sea water is in my mind even more difficult to ignore. That in mind:
Could you trick the sheet into _thinking_ it is being _brushed_ by copper chloride whilst it's actually being brushed by sea water?
Perhaps and additive in the ink, or layers of inks with different compositions? A catalysts of some kind (here I get out of my depth) that _tranforms_ the _sodium_ stuff into the coppery stuff?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: actually mate - so far the only real comparative we have is sodium chloride versus copper chloride. Sea water has more in it than just sodium chloride. Plus if the idea is right and copper ions with 2 positives produce such a dramatic increase how would 3 plus ions fare? - ferric chloride for example - what we are looking at is a divergence - the copper/ferric energy cell for land use and seawater as a cheap generator for centrally distributed power. What is important in power generation isn't so much the amount of power a system can generate but rather the cost per kilowatt and given the proposed system is basically a carbon paint coat the cost per kilowatt is going to be tiny and that is where it is likely to win out. There are better systems but the cost per kilowatt is very much higher making them unusable - lithium is a rare, expensive and getting rarer material.

replies.author: @FredGandt
replies.text:
"Indeed" on all counts :-)
Unfortunately my knowledge of the physics/chemistry crossover is somewhat lacking.
I was wondering whilst walking the dog if any gasses could be useful in place of the liquids?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Fred Gandt
do you know - i have no idea but as gasses and liquids are both working fluids in principle i can't see why not

replies.author: @FredGandt
replies.text:
Just a fanciful thought -
Electric pulse jet? :-)

author: @VAXKILLEDMYDAD
text:
For anyone interested in the original paper, where an LED is powered by such a device. Check this out.

http://www.rexresearch.com/kaner/1203.0161.pdf

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: brilliant post mate - thanks for the link

replies.author: @VAXKILLEDMYDAD
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith My pleasure! I'm sure the Lovell device can also run an LED, and it's next weeks task to prove it. Just waiting for the ZnO, Cu tape and PVA glue to arrive. I'm also waiting for epoxy, because I want to seal it inside and check it will still continue to run under load for an extended period. If it does, then that would go a long way to convincing me that this is generating elecy from the ambient heat, and not just another galvanic battery.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
MrSkypelessons
we live in interesting times lol

replies.author: @pozytywniezakrecony151
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith  Hmm so desired conditions are: fluid in motion(give me that electron!), high temperature(particles in motion, again more often meeting graphene to give back electron) and high surface area of graphene(again more often..) inside that fluid. Seems like CuCl2 can work up to 1000C and then it decomposes. Fair enough - I guess, sun->heat->electricity. Interesting paper.

author: @slackerbelmont
text: Nice experiment. I do wonder about ways to agitate the water, since in this video you are using electricity to agitate the water and getting a negative return from the graphene, would it be possible to say create screens something with large strips that will let sea water pass through, put many layers inside of a large tube, put said tube into the ocean and let the rise and fall of the water do the work. something like an ocean battery. I know you are working with early concept stages, but without an engery free way of agitating water you will always need to overcome what you are putting in. Thanks again for the content.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes sure - like I say this is just an investigation - I am not trying to build anything here - just look at the odd properties
